narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 84% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates overall. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your classmates—far below the middle-upper range typical for those who passed the bar—pushing the prediction heavily toward expecting failure. Your Year 1 decile in the second lowest group also contributed noticeably in the same direction, signaling early academic struggles that echoed patterns in students who later struggled on the exam. Even your LSAT score of 30 played a role here, as it fell well short of the average around 37 seen among bar passers, further nudging the model toward a failure outlook. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average for successful students and your full-time enrollment status aligned with most who passed, these weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Your lower-middle family income bracket, though not a top driver, is less common among bar passers, who more often come from middle to upper-middle backgrounds. Compared to typical accepted students, who tend to rank in higher deciles early and throughout undergrad, show stronger entrance exam performance, and hail from more stable financial situations, your profile raised red flags in the areas the model weighs most for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's just how your specifics stacked up against historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic performance through additional coursework or tutoring to climb those decile rankings, and exploring programs with bar prep support tailored for students like you. You might also gather stronger letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience or seek feedback from advisors on bridging these gaps. With targeted improvements, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift the odds in your favor.
